title: Boundary Waters Wilderness Protection and Pollution Prevention Act
short_title: Boundary Waters Wilderness Protection and Pollution Prevention Act
plain_summary: This bill protects about 225,000 acres of land and water in Minnesota's Superior National Forest from most types of mining. The protected area is near the Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness, which is a popular place for camping and canoeing. The law stops companies from mining for things like copper because mining could pollute the clean water and hurt the forest. The Forest Service can still allow some mining for sand, granite, and iron ore if it won't harm the water, air, or forest animals.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Natural Resources.
subjects: Wilderness and natural areas, wildlife refuges, wild rivers, habitats

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Boundary Waters Wilderness Protection and Pollution Prevention Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found these facts about the Boundary Waters area. The Boundary Waters Canoe Area Wilderness is a one million ninety thousand acre protected wilderness area inside the Superior National Forest. It was first protected by the Wilderness Act in nineteen sixty four. The Forest Service runs this wilderness area. It has almost two thousand clean lakes that range from ten acres to ten thousand acres in size. It also has more than one thousand two hundred miles of canoe routes. The area contains one thousand five hundred places with important history including old Ojibwe village sites and Native American rock art. It has one hundred fifty miles of land and water on the border with Canada. In nineteen seventy eight Congress passed another law about this area. That law removed activities that did not belong there and banned mining in the wilderness and on two hundred twenty thousand more acres of the Superior National Forest. The law gave guidance on how to protect and improve the lakes, waterways, and forests so people could better enjoy the unique landscape and wildlife. Three Native American tribes have rights to hunt and fish in this entire northeast part of Minnesota including the Boundary Waters. These rights come from an eighteen fifty four treaty. The tribes are the Grand Portage Band of Lake Superior Chippewa, the Fond du Lac Band of Lake Superior Chippewa, and the Bois Forte Band of Chippewa. All these tribes have a legal right to protect natural resources and the Forest Service must help maintain what the treaty promises. The Rainy River Watershed is inside the Superior National Forest. This forest holds twenty percent of all the fresh water in the entire National Forest System. The Rainy River Watershed starts in northeastern Minnesota and flows north through the Boundary Waters and Voyageurs National Park into Canada along the shared border. These waters between countries are controlled by a treaty from nineteen oh nine. That treaty says boundary waters cannot be polluted on either side if it would hurt health or property on the other side. The waters in the Boundary Waters and Voyageurs National Park are classified as Outstanding Resource Value Waters under federal and state law. This means making the water quality worse is not allowed. Mining creates a risk called acid mine drainage. This happens when sulfide minerals touch air and water and create sulfuric acid. This acid lowers the pH of water and pulls out harmful metals like copper, zinc, lead, cadmium, iron, and nickel. Acid mine runoff from sulfide ore copper mining gets into groundwater, rivers, streams, and lakes. This hurts water life, makes water quality worse, and can cause severe environmental damage. A reviewed study looked at water quality impacts from fourteen operating copper sulfide mines in the United States. The study found that one hundred percent of the mines had pipeline spills or accidental releases. Thirteen mines had failures of water collection and treatment systems that were supposed to control contaminated mine seepage. These failures caused significant negative water quality impacts. Mining copper and other metals in sulfide bearing ore on federal lands in the Superior National Forest within the Rainy River Watershed poses a direct and long term threat. Sulfide ore mining contamination threatens the clean water and air quality and healthy forest habitat of the Boundary Waters and Voyageurs National Park. Contamination of the air, water, and forest habitat from mining copper, nickel, platinum, palladium, gold, and silver on federal lands in the Rainy River Watershed would put important things at risk. It would risk the nationally recognized natural resources of the area. It would also risk the region's tourism industry. If the area is protected by preventing mining, it would grow by one thousand five hundred to four thousand six hundred more jobs. It would also bring one hundred million to nine hundred million dollars more income over the next twenty years compared to if mining were allowed. In two thousand sixteen the Forest Service issued a Record of Decision. It found unacceptable the risk that developing a copper nickel sulfide ore mine in the same watershed as the Boundary Waters might cause serious and irreplaceable harm to this unique and irreplaceable wilderness area. The Forest Service then proposed a twenty year mineral withdrawal of federal lands and waters in the Rainy River Watershed. In two thousand eighteen about twenty months into a twenty four month review period the Department of Agriculture suddenly canceled the withdrawal application and abandoned the Environmental Assessment. In two thousand twenty one the Forest Service started the mineral withdrawal proposal again for National Forest System lands in the Rainy River Watershed. A comprehensive Environmental Assessment was completed in two thousand twenty two. This provided extensive scientific analysis and public input supporting the Service's recommendation in favor of withdrawal. On January twenty sixth two thousand twenty three the Secretary of the Interior decided to withdraw two hundred twenty five thousand five hundred four acres of public land. This was done to protect and preserve the fragile and vital social and natural resources, ecological integrity, and wilderness values in the Rainy River watershed.

sections.title: Section 3  Withdrawal of Certain Federal Lands and Waters in the State of Minnesota
sections.text: In this law the term Map means the map attached to Public Land Order Number seven thousand nine hundred seventeen for Withdrawal of Federal Lands in Cook, Lake, and Saint Louis Counties, Minnesota. This map was published in the Federal Register dated January thirty first two thousand twenty three. Except for some exceptions and subject to valid existing rights, about two hundred twenty five thousand five hundred four acres of federal land and waters in the Rainy River Watershed of the Superior National Forest in Minnesota are hereby withdrawn. These lands are located on the Map and described in the Federal Register Notice dated January thirty first two thousand twenty three. They are withdrawn from all forms of entry, appropriation, and disposal under the public land laws. They are also withdrawn from location, entry, and patent under the mining laws. They are also withdrawn from operation of the mineral leasing, mineral materials, and geothermal leasing laws. Any land or interest in land within the area shown on the Map that is bought by the United States after this law is enacted shall immediately be withdrawn according to this section when it is bought. The Chief of the Forest Service is allowed to permit the removal of sand, gravel, granite, iron ore, and taconite from national forest system lands within the area shown on the Map. This is only allowed if the Chief determines that the removal will not hurt the water quality, air quality, and health of the forest habitat within the Rainy River Watershed. The Map shall be kept on file and made available for public inspection in the appropriate offices of the Forest Service and the Bureau of Land Management.
